title: In the Beginning Was the Word
subtitle: Before Mary, before Lucifer, before the world: the eternal birth of the Son from the Father

stillness: Of his birth, who shall tell?
The divine generation is wholly unutterable.
Do not ask what cannot be unbound.
gospel: In the beginning was the Word,
and the Word was with God,
This was in the beginning with God.
All things were made through him,
and without him was nothing made.
behold: He calls him not the Son, but the Word.
For the spoken word is a word only loosely, by likeness.
The true word is the inner conceiving of the mind, before any voice clothes it.
So the Son proceeds from the Father by an eternal birth,
and with him, and in him, remains, by the oneness of their nature,
as the thought born from the mind abides within the mind, and does not depart from it.
The Word was with God, as one with another:
for always the Son is in the Father, always the Father in the Son.
nothing is in God that is not God.
He did not begin to be in Mary;
in the beginning he was, that is, in the Father —
the Principle without a principle, and the Son, the Principle from the Principle.
consider: The Father never was without the Son.
Never without the Word, never without his Power, never without his Wisdom could he be.
We call him Father because he has a Son: for this is what it is to be a Father — to have a Son.
And because the Father who utters and conceives the Word is from eternity,
the Word he begets by uttering is begotten not in the beginning of time —
of which it is said, In the beginning God created heaven and earth —
but in the beginning of eternity:
With thee is the Principle in the day of thy strength, in the splendours of the Saints;
from the womb, that is, from my substance, before Lucifer, before the world's making, I begot thee.
When it is said, This day have I begotten thee, it means the day of eternity, which embraces every day.
But how the Father himself begot the Word — since it cannot be unbound, it must not be asked.
For though it is said the Son is begotten of the Father, yet how, neither prophet knew, nor Angel.
From all this we are taught, in the moral sense:
that the beginning of our every intention must be God.
Would you know whether your every act, inward and outward, is divine —
whether God himself works it in you, and through him it is done?
See if the end of your intention is God.
If it is, then your act is divine:
for the beginning and the end are one and the same, namely God.
prayer: Lord Jesus, eternal Word of the Father,
you were in the beginning, the Principle from the Principle.
With thee is the Principle in the day of thy strength, in the splendours of the Saints;
from the womb, before Lucifer, you were begotten.
Be the beginning of my every intention,
and the end toward which I move,
that my acts may be divine,
since the beginning and the end are one and the same — you, my God.

latinJewel.latin: Numquam enim Pater sine Filio fuit, numquam sine Verbo, numquam sine Virtute, numquam sine Sapientia esse potuit.
latinJewel.english: For never was the Father without the Son; never without the Word, never without his Power, never without his Wisdom could he be.
